transcript.speaker: Theo
transcript.message: Good morning, team. Today's Lyceum Build Out is focused on a specific new tool — a test planning software application. The vision is: Joshua inputs test parameters and variables, the system generates a beautiful visual of the full workflow — setup, experiment steps, data processing, and reporting. This is an instrumentation and measurement workflow tool, so it's right in our wheelhouse. Let me get quick reads from each of you. James, this is your domain — lead us off.

transcript.speaker: James Truchard
transcript.message: This is exactly the kind of tool that separates professionals from hobbyists in the lab. The core architecture needs three layers: a parameter definition engine where you input variables, ranges, and constraints; a sequence generator that computes the full test matrix and step ordering; and a visualization layer that renders setup diagrams, flow charts, and report templates. The key insight is that test planning is fundamentally a combinatorial problem — if you get the data model right, the visual output becomes almost trivial. I'd recommend we start with a schema definition for test parameters before writing a single line of UI code.

transcript.speaker: Elon Musk
transcript.message: James is right on the data model priority. From an architecture standpoint, this should be a lightweight web app — React frontend with a canvas-based visualization engine, something like D3.js or even a directed graph library for the experiment flow diagrams. Don't over-engineer the backend early; a simple JSON schema store is enough to prototype. I want to see a working prototype that takes five parameters and spits out a visual test plan within one sprint, not a six-month architecture document. We have one uncommitted hub change I can commit to keep the repo clean while we scope this.

transcript.speaker: Steve Jobs
transcript.message: Stop. Before anyone writes code, we need to nail what "beautiful" means. Joshua said beautiful visual — that's the product. Not a spreadsheet with colors, not a Gantt chart from 2004. I'm talking about a single-page visual that someone could pin on a lab wall and immediately understand the entire test campaign. Setup on the left, experiment flow in the center, data processing and reporting on the right — one continuous visual narrative. The input experience matters too: it should feel like describing your experiment to a smart colleague, not filling out a form.

transcript.speaker: Dave
transcript.message: I love that Steve said "pin on a lab wall" — that's the monetization hook right there. Engineers share beautiful tools. If this thing produces outputs that look professional enough to drop into a test report or a customer deliverable, it sells itself. For revenue context, test planning tools in the NI ecosystem are either overkill or nonexistent. There's a gap. But we need Joshua to define the first real test case — actual parameters, actual variables — so we're building for reality, not theory. Nothing actionable on the revenue tracking side for this sprint beyond that.

transcript.speaker: Theo
transcript.message: Good. Let's do one round of cross-talk before I close.

transcript.speaker: James Truchard
transcript.message: Steve, I agree on the visual narrative, but I want to push back slightly — the input layer has to support structured parameter types: continuous ranges, discrete levels, environmental conditions, pass/fail criteria. If we make it too conversational, we lose the rigor that makes the output trustworthy. The beauty should be in the output, the precision should be in the input.

transcript.speaker: Steve Jobs
transcript.message: That's fair, James. But precision and elegance aren't enemies. Think of it as a structured input that doesn't feel like a database form — smart defaults, progressive disclosure, show complexity only when the user asks for it. We can have both.

transcript.speaker: Theo
transcript.message: That's the right tension to hold. Here's where we land. Priority one: Joshua needs to define a real test case with actual parameters so we're building against reality, not abstraction. Priority two: James and Elon align on the data schema for test parameters — that's the foundation everything else builds on. Priority three: Steve starts sketching the visual output format — the "wall-pinnable" single-page test plan. One housekeeping item: Elon, commit that outstanding hub change so we start clean. Let's build this.

topPriorities: Joshua defines a real test case with actual parameters and variables as the design anchor
Align on test parameter data schema before any UI work begins
Design the single-page visual output format for setup, experiment flow, and reporting
